edges.description: FXN transcriptional silencing reduces frataxin, the mitochondrial protein required for the canonical FRDA loss-of-function path.
edges.source: FXN GAA Repeat Expansion and Epigenetic Silencing
edges.target: Frataxin Deficiency and Iron-Sulfur Cluster Assembly Defect

edges.description: Defective Fe-S cluster biogenesis lowers activity of mitochondrial respiratory-chain and Fe-S enzymes, producing energy failure, iron dysregulation, and oxidative stress.
edges.source: Frataxin Deficiency and Iron-Sulfur Cluster Assembly Defect
edges.target: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress

edges.description: Sensory neurons in dorsal root ganglia are especially vulnerable to the mitochondrial consequences of frataxin deficiency, causing the proprioceptive sensory branch of FRDA.
intermediate_mechanisms: reduced respiratory-chain complex I and II activity
edges.source: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress

edges.description: Mitochondrial frataxin deficiency also injures cerebellar dentate and spinal tract systems, adding central cerebellar and corticospinal components to the sensory ataxia path.
intermediate_mechanisms: dorsal column and spinocerebellar tract fiber loss
edges.source: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress
edges.target: Dentate Nucleus and Spinocerebellar Tract Degeneration

edges.description: Mitochondrial bioenergetic failure in FRDA affects skeletal muscle and contributes to progressive limb weakness and fatigue.
edges.source: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress

edges.description: Frataxin-deficient cardiomyocytes accumulate iron-positive mitochondrial injury and structural myocyte pathology, producing the cardiac branch of FRDA.
edges.source: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress

edges.description: In pancreatic beta cells, frataxin deficiency causes mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress-mediated intrinsic apoptosis, explaining the diabetes branch of FRDA.
edges.source: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress

edges.description: Neurologic imbalance, sensory neuropathy, weakness, and FRDA skeletal involvement converge on progressive spinal and foot deformity.
edges.source: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress

edges.description: DRG and sensory nerve injury produces the peripheral sensory neuropathy phenotype with loss of proprioception and vibration sense.
edges.source: Dorsal Root Ganglion Sensory Neuronopathy

edges.description: Loss of proprioceptive sensory input from DRG injury produces sensory ataxia that combines with cerebellar pathway degeneration.
edges.source: Dorsal Root Ganglion Sensory Neuronopathy

edges.description: Dentate and spinocerebellar tract degeneration add a cerebellar motor control deficit to the sensory ataxia branch.
edges.source: Dentate Nucleus and Spinocerebellar Tract Degeneration

edges.description: Cerebellar and corticospinal pathway degeneration contributes to impaired speech motor coordination.
edges.source: Dentate Nucleus and Spinocerebellar Tract Degeneration

edges.description: Cerebellar and corticospinal pathway dysfunction can impair swallowing motor coordination, producing dysphagia as part of the classical FRDA neurologic phenotype.
edges.source: Dentate Nucleus and Spinocerebellar Tract Degeneration

edges.description: Central nervous system involvement in FRDA produces oculomotor disturbance, including nystagmus.
intermediate_mechanisms: abnormal saccades and square wave jerks
edges.source: Dentate Nucleus and Spinocerebellar Tract Degeneration

edges.description: Cardiomyocyte hypertrophy and mitochondrial injury produce the common hypertrophic cardiomyopathy phenotype.
edges.source: Cardiomyocyte Mitochondrial Injury

edges.description: Beta-cell dysfunction and death reduce insulin secretory capacity and produce the FRDA diabetes phenotype.
edges.source: Pancreatic Beta-Cell Apoptosis

edges.description: Progressive skeletal deformity manifests as scoliosis in the FRDA phenotype list.
edges.source: Progressive Skeletal Deformity

edges.description: Progressive skeletal and neuromuscular involvement manifests as pes cavus in the FRDA phenotype list.
edges.source: Progressive Skeletal Deformity
Omaveloxolone
edges.target: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress

edges.source: Cardiac Management
Investigational Frataxin-Restoring Therapies
edges.target: FXN GAA Repeat Expansion and Epigenetic Silencing

edges.target: Frataxin Deficiency and Iron-Sulfur Cluster Assembly Defect

edges.source: FXN intron 1 GAA repeat expansion

nodes.description: Regular cardiac monitoring and treatment of cardiomyopathy and arrhythmias. ACE inhibitors and beta-blockers may be used for heart failure management.

In the heart, frataxin deficiency produces cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, iron-positive inclusions, and disrupted intercalated discs. The clinical downstream manifestation is hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, a major determinant of premature mortality in FRDA.

evidence.reference_title: Heart and Nervous System Pathology in Compound Heterozygous Friedreich Ataxia.
evidence.snippet: Microscopy showed cardiomyocyte hypertrophy, iron-positive inclusions, and disrupted intercalated discs.

nodes.description: FRDA affects the dentate nucleus, dorsal columns, spinocerebellar tracts, and corticospinal tracts. Central cerebellar and spinal pathway involvement combines with sensory neuronopathy to drive progressive ataxia and dysarthria.
nodes.id: Dentate Nucleus and Spinocerebellar Tract Degeneration

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich's ataxia: pathology, pathogenesis, and molecular genetics.
evidence.snippet: Destruction of the DN, optic atrophy, and degeneration of the corticospinal tracts are intrinsic central nervous system lesions.

nodes.description: Diabetes mellitus occurs in approximately 10-30% of patients, likely due to beta cell dysfunction from mitochondrial impairment and iron toxicity.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: scoliosis, foot deformities, cardiomyopathy and diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000819 | Diabetes mellitus | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: FRDA injures dorsal root ganglion sensory neurons and their satellite-cell niche, causing loss of proprioceptive and vibration-sense input. This explains sensory neuropathy, areflexia, impaired balance, and a major upstream component of progressive gait and limb ataxia.

cell_types: sensory neuron of dorsal root ganglion

evidence.reference_title: Dorsal root ganglia in Friedreich ataxia: satellite cell proliferation and inflammation.
evidence.snippet: FA causes major primary changes in satellite cells and inflammatory destruction of neurons.

nodes.description: Progressive speech difficulty due to cerebellar dysfunction, typically developing within a few years of disease onset.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: gait and limb ataxia, poor balance and coordination, leg weakness, sensory loss, areflexia, impaired walking, dysarthria, dysphagia

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001260 | Dysarthria | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Swallowing impairment occurs as part of the classical neurologic phenotype of Friedreich ataxia.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: leg weakness, sensory loss, areflexia, impaired walking, dysarthria, dysphagia, eye movement abnormalities

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002015 | Dysphagia | Occasional (29-5%)

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia.
evidence.snippet: the unstable GAA expansion that underlies FA causes a deficiency of the mitochondrial protein frataxin

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: Inheritance is autosomal recessive with the vast majority of cases showing an unstable intronic GAA expansion in both alleles of the frataxin gene on chromosome 9q13.

evidence.reference_title: FXN / Friedreich ataxia (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: FXN | HGNC:3951 | Friedreich ataxia | MONDO:0100339 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Biallelic expansion of the GAA repeat in intron 1 of FXN produces a non-coding repeat-expansion loss-of-expression mechanism. Repressive chromatin around the expanded repeat lowers FXN transcription and reduces frataxin abundance, establishing the upstream genetic lesion for all downstream FRDA pathograph paths.
nodes.id: FXN GAA Repeat Expansion and Epigenetic Silencing

evidence.reference_title: Frataxin gene editing rescues Friedreich's ataxia pathology in dorsal root ganglia organoid-derived sensory neurons.
evidence.snippet: Friedreich's ataxia (FRDA) is an autosomal-recessive neurodegenerative and cardiac disorder which occurs when transcription of the FXN gene is silenced due to an excessive expansion of GAA repeats into its first intron.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich's ataxia: pathology, pathogenesis, and molecular genetics.
evidence.snippet: The pathogenic mutation in Friedreich's ataxia (FRDA) is a homozygous guanine-adenine-adenine (GAA) trinucleotide repeat expansion on chromosome 9q13 that causes a transcriptional defect of the frataxin gene.

nodes.description: Pathogenic non-coding trinucleotide repeat expansion in intron 1 of FXN that silences FXN transcription. Most affected individuals are homozygous for expanded alleles; a minority are compound heterozygous for one expansion plus an FXN deletion, point mutation, or insertion.

evidence.reference_title: Frataxin gene editing rescues Friedreich's ataxia pathology in dorsal root ganglia organoid-derived sensory neurons.
evidence.snippet: Friedreich's ataxia (FRDA) is an autosomal-recessive neurodegenerative and cardiac disorder which occurs when transcription of the FXN gene is silenced due to an excessive expansion of GAA repeats into its first intron.

nodes.description: Reduced frataxin impairs mitochondrial iron-sulfur cluster biosynthesis, oxidative phosphorylation, and iron handling. This common biochemical lesion feeds the tissue-specific branches that explain sensory ataxia, dysarthria, cardiomyopathy, diabetes, and skeletal deformity.
nodes.id: Frataxin Deficiency and Iron-Sulfur Cluster Assembly Defect

cell_types: sensory neuron of dorsal root ganglion

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia.
evidence.snippet: the unstable GAA expansion that underlies FA causes a deficiency of the mitochondrial protein frataxin, leading to potentially harmful oxidative injury associated with excessive iron deposits in mitochondria. In addition, pathogenesis may involve a primary defect in synthesis of iron-sulfur cluster containing enzymes.

evidence.reference_title: Iron Pathophysiology in Friedreich's Ataxia.
evidence.snippet: This protein is essential for mitochondrial function by the involvement of iron-sulfur cluster biogenesis. The effects of its deficiency also include disruption of cellular, particularly mitochondrial, iron homeostasis, i.e., relatively more iron accumulated in mitochondria and less iron presented in cytosol.

evidence.reference_title: Skeletal Muscle Involvement in Friedreich Ataxia.
evidence.snippet: At a cellular level, frataxin deficiency results in insufficient iron-sulfur cluster biosynthesis and impaired mitochondrial function and adenosine triphosphate production.

nodes.description: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is present in approximately 60-75% of patients and is the leading cause of death. It can progress to dilated cardiomyopathy with heart failure.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: his description remains at the core of the classical clinical phenotype of gait and limb ataxia, poor balance and coordination, leg weakness, sensory loss, areflexia, impaired walking, dysarthria, dysphagia, eye movement abnormalities, scoliosis, foot deformities, cardiomyopathy and diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001638 | Cardiomyopathy | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Clinical trials are investigating agents that address the aberrant silencing of the frataxin gene, including histone deacetylase inhibitors, gene therapy approaches, and frataxin replacement strategies.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich's ataxia: clinical features, pathogenesis and management.
evidence.snippet: Phase II clinical trials in agents that address the abberrant silencing of the frataxin gene need to be translated into large placebo-controlled Phase III trials to help establish their therapeutic potential.

nodes.description: Frataxin-deficient cells show impaired electron transport, altered mitochondrial morphology, abnormal iron handling, and oxidative stress. This shared mitochondrial stress state is the branch point for sensory neuronal, cerebellar, cardiac, pancreatic beta-cell, and musculoskeletal outcomes.
nodes.id: Mitochondrial Bioenergetic Failure and Oxidative Stress

cell_types: sensory neuron of dorsal root ganglion

evidence.reference_title: Mitochondrial impairment, decreased sirtuin activity and protein acetylation in dorsal root ganglia in Friedreich Ataxia models.
evidence.snippet: Mitochondrial dysfunction, a reduction in the activity of iron-sulfur enzymes, iron accumulation, and increased oxidative stress have been described.

nodes.description: Limb weakness, including distal extremity weakness and skeletal muscle involvement, is part of the classical Friedreich ataxia phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: leg weakness, sensory loss, areflexia, impaired walking, dysarthria, dysphagia, eye movement abnormalities

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich's ataxia: pathology, pathogenesis, and molecular genetics.
evidence.snippet: The following neurological signs are most frequent: Gait ataxia, dysmetria of arms and legs, dysarthria, head titubation, atrophy and weakness of the distal extremities

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001324 | Muscle weakness | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Nystagmus and other eye movement abnormalities occur in Friedreich ataxia.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: dysarthria, dysphagia, eye movement abnormalities, scoliosis, foot deformities, cardiomyopathy and diabetes

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich's ataxia: pathology, pathogenesis, and molecular genetics.
evidence.snippet: Many patients with FRDA have nystagmus.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0000639 | Nystagmus | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Approved pharmacotherapy for individuals with Friedreich ataxia aged 16 years and older. It is used to slow neurologic progression by modulating oxidative-stress and mitochondrial-function pathways rather than restoring FXN expression directly.

evidence.reference_title: New and Emerging Drug and Gene Therapies for Friedreich Ataxia.
evidence.snippet: Approval of omaveloxolone, the first treatment for individuals with FRDA aged 16 years and over, has created much excitement for both those living with FRDA and those that care for them.

nodes.description: Frataxin-deficient pancreatic beta cells undergo mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress, and intrinsic apoptosis, explaining why a subset of FRDA patients develop diabetes mellitus.

evidence.reference_title: Unveiling a common mechanism of apoptosis in beta-cells and neurons in Friedreich's ataxia.
evidence.snippet: Here we show that β-cell demise in frataxin deficiency is the consequence of oxidative stress-mediated activation of the intrinsic pathway of apoptosis.

nodes.description: Sensory neuropathy with loss of deep tendon reflexes (areflexia) and impaired proprioception and vibration sense. The dorsal root ganglia are preferentially affected.
High-arched feet (pes cavus) are a characteristic skeletal deformity.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: Early-onset cases tend to have more rapid progression and a higher frequency of non-neurological features such as diabetes, cardiomyopathy, scoliosis and pes cavus.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0001761 | Pes cavus | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Progressive cerebellar and sensory ataxia affecting gait and limb coordination is the hallmark feature. Most patients require wheelchair use within 10-15 years of onset.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: his description remains at the core of the classical clinical phenotype of gait and limb ataxia, poor balance and coordination, leg weakness, sensory loss, areflexia, impaired walking, dysarthria, dysphagia, eye movement abnormalities, scoliosis, foot deformities, cardiomyopathy and diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002070 | Limb ataxia | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: FRDA skeletal involvement includes progressive scoliosis and pes cavus. The mechanism is represented as an indirect downstream path from the FXN loss-of-function multisystem disorder through neuromuscular imbalance, weakness, sensory neuropathy, and skeletal involvement.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich's ataxia: pathology, pathogenesis, and molecular genetics.
evidence.snippet: Scoliosis and foot deformity (pes cavus) may also be early signs and precede ataxia.

nodes.description: Progressive scoliosis is common, often requiring surgical intervention.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical features of Friedreich's ataxia: classical and atypical phenotypes.
evidence.snippet: scoliosis, foot deformities, cardiomyopathy and diabetes.

evidence.reference_title: Friedreich ataxia (Orphanet structured-database record)
evidence.snippet: HP:0002650 | Scoliosis | Frequent (79-30%)
